examples.title: Algorithm: GPIO Input/Output Control
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating GPIO pin control for reading sensors and controlling actuators
examples.code:
ALGORITHM GPIO_Control
INPUT: pin_number, mode (INPUT/OUTPUT), value (for OUTPUT)
OUTPUT: digital_value (for INPUT)

BEGIN
 # Initialize GPIO interface
 CALL Initialize_GPIO()
 
 # Configure pin numbering scheme
 SET numbering_mode TO BCM # Use Broadcom pin numbering
 CALL Set_GPIO_Mode(numbering_mode)
 
 # Setup pin direction
 IF mode = INPUT THEN
 CALL GPIO_SetDirection(pin_number, INPUT)
 ELSE
 CALL GPIO_SetDirection(pin_number, OUTPUT)
 END IF
 
 # Main control loop
 WHILE running = TRUE DO
 IF mode = INPUT THEN
 # Read digital input from sensor/button
 SET digital_value TO GPIO_Read(pin_number)
 IF digital_value = HIGH THEN
 PRINT "Input detected ON"
 ELSE
 PRINT "Input detected OFF"
 END IF
 ELSE
 # Write output to LED/actuator
 IF value = HIGH THEN
 CALL GPIO_Write(pin_number, HIGH)
 PRINT "Output set ON"
 ELSE
 CALL GPIO_Write(pin_number, LOW)
 PRINT "Output set OFF"
 END IF
 END IF
 WAIT for 1 second
 END WHILE
 
 # Cleanup GPIO pins on exit
 CALL GPIO_Cleanup()
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the fundamental GPIO control flow: initialization, pin configuration, input/output operations, and proper cleanup. It demonstrates both reading from sensors (INPUT mode) and controlling actuators (OUTPUT mode). The algorithm uses BCM numbering which maps to the SoC's GPIO pins.

examples.title: Python: GPIO LED Blink with RPi.GPIO
examples.description: Complete Python program to blink an LED connected to GPIO pin 18 using PWM for variable brightness control
examples.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Raspberry Pi GPIO LED Control with PWM
This example demonstrates GPIO output control with Pulse Width Modulation
to create variable brightness effects on an LED.
"""

import RPi.GPIO as GPIO # Import GPIO library
import time

# Pin configuration using BCM numbering
LED_PIN = 18 # Physical pin 12, BCM pin 18

def setup():
 """Initialize GPIO settings"""
 GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM) # Use BCM pin numbering (not physical)
 GPIO.setwarnings(False) # Disable GPIO warnings
 GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT) # Set LED_PIN as output
 print(f"GPIO {LED_PIN} initialized as output")

def blink_led(duration_on=1, duration_off=1, iterations=5):
 """
 Simple LED blink function
 
 Args:
 duration_on: Time LED stays ON (seconds)
 duration_off: Time LED stays OFF (seconds)
 iterations: Number of blink cycles
 """
 for i in range(iterations):
 GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH) # Turn LED ON
 print("LED: ON")
 time.sleep(duration_on)
 
 GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW) # Turn LED OFF
 print("LED: OFF")
 time.sleep(duration_off)

def pwm_brightness(frequency=100, duty_cycle=50, duration=5):
 """
 LED brightness control using PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)
 
 Args:
 frequency: PWM frequency in Hz
 duty_cycle: Percentage of time signal is HIGH (0-100)
 duration: How long to run PWM (seconds)
 """
 # Create PWM instance with frequency
 pwm = GPIO.PWM(LED_PIN, frequency)
 pwm.start(duty_cycle) # Start PWM with initial duty cycle
 
 print(f"PWM running at {frequency}Hz with {duty_cycle}% brightness")
 time.sleep(duration)
 
 # Fade effect: gradually increase and decrease brightness
 for duty in range(0, 101, 5):
 pwm.ChangeDutyCycle(duty)
 time.sleep(0.05)
 
 for duty in range(100, -1, -5):
 pwm.ChangeDutyCycle(duty)
 time.sleep(0.05)
 
 pwm.stop() # Stop PWM
 print("PWM fade complete")

def read_button_input(button_pin=17):
 """
 Read digital input from a button/sensor on GPIO pin 17
 
 Args:
 button_pin: GPIO pin connected to button
 """
 GPIO.setup(button_pin, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_DOWN)
 print(f"Reading button input on GPIO {button_pin}")
 
 # Read button state
 button_state = GPIO.input(button_pin)
 
 if button_state == GPIO.HIGH:
 print("Button PRESSED")
 else:
 print("Button NOT pressed")
 
 return button_state

def cleanup():
 """Clean up GPIO settings on exit"""
 GPIO.cleanup()
 print("GPIO cleanup complete")

# Main execution
if __name__ == "__main__":
 try:
 setup()
 
 # Demonstrate simple blinking
 print("\n--- Simple LED Blink ---")
 blink_led(duration_on=0.5, duration_off=0.5, iterations=3)
 
 # Demonstrate PWM brightness control
 print("\n--- PWM Brightness Control ---")
 pwm_brightness(frequency=100, duty_cycle=50, duration=2)
 
 # Demonstrate button input reading
 print("\n--- Button Input Reading ---")
 read_button_input(button_pin=17)
 
 except KeyboardInterrupt:
 print("\nInterrupted by user")
 finally:
 cleanup()
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates fundamental GPIO operations on Raspberry Pi: setting pin modes, digital output (LED control), PWM for analog-like brightness control, and digital input reading from buttons/sensors. The RPi.GPIO library provides the core interface to the Raspberry Pi's GPIO pins. Key concepts include BCM pin numbering, GPIO.HIGH/LOW for digital output, and GPIO.PWM for variable control. The cleanup() function is essential to reset pins on program exit.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for n iterations of blink loop

examples.title: Python: I2C Communication with Temperature Sensor
examples.description: Python implementation demonstrating I2C protocol to communicate with sensors like TMP102 or BMP280 using the smbus2 library
examples.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Raspberry Pi I2C Communication Example
Demonstrates reading data from I2C sensors (TMP102 temperature sensor)
using the smbus2 library for SMBus protocol access.
"""

import smbus2 as smbus # I2C communication library
import time
import struct

# I2C Configuration
I2C_BUS = 1 # I2C bus number (/dev/i2c-1 on Pi 3, 4, Zero W)
TMP102_ADDRESS = 0x48 # Default I2C address for TMP102

# TMP102 Register addresses
TEMP_REGISTER = 0x00 # Temperature data register
CONFIG_REGISTER = 0x01 # Configuration register

class TMP102Sensor:
 """Class to interface with TMP102 temperature sensor via I2C"""
 
 def __init__(self, bus_number=I2C_BUS, address=TMP102_ADDRESS):
 """
 Initialize I2C sensor connection
 
 Args:
 bus_number: I2C bus number (usually 1)
 address: 7-bit I2C device address
 """
 self.bus = smbus.SMBus(bus_number)
 self.address = address
 print(f"I2C sensor initialized at address 0x{address:02X} on bus {bus_number}")
 
 def read_temperature_celsius(self):
 """
 Read temperature from TMP102 sensor
 
 Returns:
 float: Temperature in Celsius
 """
 # Read 2 bytes from temperature register
 data = self.bus.read_i2c_block_data(self.address, TEMP_REGISTER, 2)
 
 # Convert to temperature (12-bit resolution, MSB first)
 # Combine bytes (first byte is MSB, second is LSB)
 raw_temp = (data[0] << 4) | (data[1] >> 4)
 
 # Convert to Celsius (12-bit resolution, 0.0625°C per LSB)
 # Handle negative temperatures using two's complement
 if raw_temp >= 2048:
 raw_temp = raw_temp - 4096
 
 temperature = raw_temp * 0.0625
 return temperature
 
 def read_temperature_fahrenheit(self):
 """Read temperature in Fahrenheit"""
 celsius = self.read_temperature_celsius()
 return (celsius * 9/5) + 32
 
 def close(self):
 """Close I2C bus connection"""
 self.bus.close()


def scan_i2c_devices():
 """
 Scan and list all connected I2C devices
 Useful for debugging and finding device addresses
 """
 bus = smbus.SMBus(I2C_BUS)
 devices = []
 
 print("Scanning I2C bus for devices...")
 print("Address (hex) | Device Found")
 print("-" * 35)
 
 # Probe all possible I2C addresses (0x03 to 0x77)
 for address in range(0x03, 0x78):
 try:
 bus.write_byte(address, 0) # Try to write to address
 devices.append(address)
 print(f"0x{address:02X} | Yes")
 except Exception:
 pass # No device at this address
 
 bus.close()
 print(f"\nFound {len(devices)} I2C device(s)")
 return devices


def i2c_write_register(address, register, value):
 """
 Write a value to a specific I2C register
 
 Args:
 address: 7-bit I2C device address
 register: 8-bit register address
 value: Byte value to write
 """
 bus = smbus.SMBus(I2C_BUS)
 try:
 bus.write_byte_data(address, register, value)
 print(f"Wrote 0x{value:02X} to register 0x{register:02X}")
 except Exception as e:
 print(f"Write error: {e}")
 finally:
 bus.close()


def main():
 """Main function demonstrating I2C temperature sensor reading"""
 print("=" * 50)
 print("Raspberry Pi I2C Temperature Sensor Example")
 print("=" * 50)
 
 # First, scan for I2C devices
 print("\n[1] I2C Device Scan:")
 devices = scan_i2c_devices()
 
 if TMP102_ADDRESS not in devices:
 print(f"\nWarning: TMP102 sensor not found at 0x{TMP102_ADDRESS:02X}")
 print("Please check wiring (SDA to pin 3, SCL to pin 5)")
 return
 
 # Create sensor object and read temperature
 print("\n[2] Reading Temperature:")
 sensor = TMP102Sensor()
 
 # Read temperature 5 times
 for i in range(5):
 temp_c = sensor.read_temperature_celsius()
 temp_f = sensor.read_temperature_fahrenheit()
 
 print(f"Reading {i+1}: {temp_c:.2f}°C = {temp_f:.2f}°F")
 time.sleep(2)
 
 sensor.close()
 print("\nI2C communication complete!")


if __name__ == "__main__":
 main()
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates I2C communication protocol on Raspberry Pi. It shows how to scan for I2C devices, read from specific registers, and convert raw sensor data to meaningful values. The I2C protocol uses two wires (SDA for data, SCL for clock) and supports multiple devices on the same bus with unique 7-bit addresses. Key concepts include SMBus protocol, register-based communication, and handling multi-byte data with bit shifting.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for n temperature readings, each I2C read is O(1)

examples.title: C: GPIO Control with sysfs Interface
examples.description: C program demonstrating GPIO control through the Linux sysfs interface, including interrupt-based button handling
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates GPIO control using the Linux sysfs interface, which is the standard way to access GPIO from C programs on Raspberry Pi running Linux. The code shows GPIO export/unexport, setting pin direction, writing digital output (LED), and interrupt-based input monitoring using POSIX threads and select(). Key concepts include file operations on /sys/class/gpio, pthread creation for concurrent monitoring, and proper cleanup with unexport.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for n blink iterations, O(1) for GPIO operations
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) constant space for main thread, O(1) for button thread
